rationale: I split Mathematics into 13 practitioner-facing subspecialties to balance coverage and usability: enough granularity so active researchers would immediately recognise their field, without overwhelming the picker. I kept classical divisions (algebra, analysis, geometry/topology, number theory, logic, combinatorics) and separated methods/applied areas that attract sizable independent literatures (PDEs, numerical analysis, optimization/control, probability/stochastic, dynamical systems, mathematical physics, math-biology). I deliberately did not split probability versus statistics — probability and stochastic processes stay in math while statistical inference is routed to Statistics & Data Science; computational complexity was excluded to Computer Science & AI. Some borderline splits (e.g., numerical PDEs vs PDE theory, optimization vs control) were maintained because practitioners normally self-identify with one or the other.

subspecialties.name: Algebra
subspecialties.tagline: Groups, rings, modules, representation theory, and structures
covers: Abstract algebraic structures, homological algebra, representation theory
Examples: group theory, ring and module theory, Lie algebras, associative/ noncommutative algebra, category-theoretic algebra
explicitly_excludes: Algebraic geometry — goes to Geometry & Topology
Computational complexity — goes to Computer Science & AI

subspecialties.name: Analysis
subspecialties.tagline: Real and complex analysis, functional analysis, operator theory
covers: Core analytic techniques: measure theory, Banach/Hilbert spaces, operator theory
Examples: complex variables, harmonic analysis, spectral theory, nonlinear functional analysis
explicitly_excludes: PDE theory — goes to Partial Differential Equations
Numerical analysis — goes to Numerical Analysis & Scientific Computing

subspecialties.name: Geometry & Topology
covers: Global and local geometry, manifold theory, algebraic/complex geometry and topology
Examples: differential geometry, symplectic geometry, algebraic geometry (as geometric practitioner), topological manifolds, knot theory
explicitly_excludes: Algebraic number theory — goes to Number Theory
Mathematical physics when physics-motivated — goes to Mathematical Physics

subspecialties.name: Number Theory
subspecialties.tagline: Arithmetic, analytic, algebraic, and computational number theory
covers: Properties of integers, arithmetic geometry, modular forms, analytic methods
Examples: prime distribution, Diophantine equations, L-functions, arithmetic geometry
explicitly_excludes: Cryptography or complexity-focused computational work — goes to Computer Science & AI
Algorithmic implementation details — goes to Numerical Analysis & Scientific Computing

subspecialties.name: Mathematical Logic & Foundations
subspecialties.tagline: Set theory, model theory, proof theory, computability theory
covers: Foundational studies of mathematics, formal systems, decidability and definability
Examples: model theory, proof complexity (theoretical), set theory, recursion theory
explicitly_excludes: Applied/formal methods in CS (e.g., program verification) — goes to Computer Science & AI
Statistical inference foundations — goes to Statistics & Data Science

subspecialties.name: Combinatorics & Graph Theory
subspecialties.tagline: Enumerative, extremal, probabilistic combinatorics and graphs
covers: Discrete structures, counting, extremal problems, graph algorithms from a combinatorial viewpoint
Examples: Ramsey theory, extremal graph theory, combinatorial design, enumerative combinatorics
explicitly_excludes: Algorithmic/complexity aspects — goes to Computer Science & AI
Network-statistics inference — goes to Statistics & Data Science

subspecialties.name: Probability & Stochastic Processes
subspecialties.tagline: Probability theory, stochastic processes, martingales, limit theorems
covers: Rigorous probability theory and stochastic analysis, long-time behaviour, stochastic calculus
Examples: Brownian motion, stochastic differential equations (theory), large deviations, random matrices (mathematical focus)
explicitly_excludes: Statistical inference and applied statistics — goes to Statistics & Data Science
Data-driven stochastic modelling/ML — goes to Computer Science & AI

subspecialties.name: Dynamical Systems & Nonlinear Analysis
subspecialties.tagline: Deterministic dynamics, chaos, ergodic theory, bifurcations
covers: Theory of ordinary and discrete dynamical systems, ergodic theory and qualitative behaviour
Examples: hyperbolic dynamics, bifurcation theory, symbolic dynamics, ergodic theorems
explicitly_excludes: Numerical simulation-focused work — goes to Numerical Analysis & Scientific Computing
Physics-driven modelling when primary outlet is physics — goes to Mathematical Physics

subspecialties.name: Partial Differential Equations (PDEs)
subspecialties.tagline: Theory of PDEs, regularity, existence, and qualitative properties
covers: Analytic and geometric PDE theory, existence/uniqueness, regularity, singularities
Examples: elliptic/parabolic/hyperbolic PDE theory, conservation laws, dispersive equations
explicitly_excludes: Numerical PDE methods — goes to Numerical Analysis & Scientific Computing
Applied modelling with engineering emphasis — goes to Applied Mathematics / relevant applied field

subspecialties.name: Numerical Analysis & Scientific Computing
subspecialties.tagline: Algorithms and error analysis for numerical simulation
covers: Design and analysis of numerical methods, high-performance scientific computing, approximation theory
Examples: finite elements, spectral methods, numerical linear algebra, computational PDEs, numerical optimization
explicitly_excludes: Pure algorithmic complexity theory — goes to Computer Science & AI
Data-science oriented machine learning implementations — goes to Computer Science & AI or Statistics & Data Science

subspecialties.name: Mathematical Physics
subspecialties.tagline: Rigorous math motivated by physical theories and models
covers: Mathematical study of physical systems: rigorous quantum theory, statistical mechanics, integrable systems
Examples: quantum field theory rigor, spectral theory of physical operators, statistical mechanics foundations
explicitly_excludes: Physics experiments or phenomenology — goes to Physics
Numerical/experimental computational physics — goes to Numerical Analysis & Scientific Computing

subspecialties.name: Optimization & Control
subspecialties.tagline: Convex/nonconvex optimization, control theory, variational methods
covers: Theory and algorithms for optimizing functions and controlling dynamical systems
Examples: convex analysis, optimal control, calculus of variations, nonsmooth optimization
explicitly_excludes: Machine learning optimization applied work — goes to Computer Science & AI
Numerical implementation-focused solver engineering — goes to Numerical Analysis & Scientific Computing

subspecialties.name: Mathematical Biology
subspecialties.tagline: Mathematical models of biological systems and processes
covers: Theoretical and mathematical analysis of biological phenomena: population dynamics, biomechanics, pattern formation
Examples: epidemiological models, reaction-diffusion modelling, theoretical ecology, mathematical neuroscience (theory-focused)
explicitly_excludes: Data-driven bioinformatics/statistics — goes to Statistics & Data Science
Experimental wet-lab biology — goes to Biology or domain-specific discipline

uncovered_concerns: Algebraic geometry sits between Algebra and Geometry & Topology; I placed it under Geometry & Topology because many practitioners identify as geometers, but some algebraic-number-theory-adjacent work could reasonably sit in Number Theory.
Computational complexity and many CS-adjacent discrete/algorithmic topics are intentionally excluded and routed to Computer Science & AI to keep MECE boundaries clean.

Applied, interdisciplinary papers (e.g., ML-heavy optimization or data-driven PDEs) can be ambiguous; routing guidance favours the primary methodological identity (theory → math subspecialty, data/ML → CS or Statistics as appropriate).
